concerts.label: New York Jazz Repertory Company: The Music of Quincy Jones

about_quotes.speaker: Harold Mabern
about_quotes.evidence: we had a good group in Chicago called the MJT + 3, young people. Frank Strozier, Bob Cranshaw, Walter Perkins, myself, Bobby Bryant, you know.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harold Mabern interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, March 31, 2010

about_quotes.speaker: Louis Hayes
about_quotes.evidence: I made a change and got a quartet at the time with Frank Strozier and Harold Mabern and Stafford James stayed there for a period of time.
about_quotes.doc_title: Louis Hayes interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 29, 2000

about_quotes.speaker: Roy Haynes
about_quotes.evidence: I think I had Frank Strozier during the time – when I was filling in.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roy Haynes — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Segal
about_quotes.evidence: in walks this group from Memphis: George Coleman, Booker Little, Frank Strozier... and they proceeded to burn the place down. And I remember George had on a big Army overcoat...
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Segal — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: He was known for really swinging and they had a hit called "Sleepy," it was a local hit in Chicago. Frank Strozier played in the band...
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jackie McLean
about_quotes.evidence: So I said, 'Yeah. Take Frank Strozier with you.' I said, 'He can play, man. From Chicago. Call him up.'
about_quotes.doc_title: Jackie McLean — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
